[JS Weird World] No.7 Quick Concept Check: Single-Threaded and Synchronous Execution

"Single-threaded" looks like one of those fancy, intimidating terms, but honestly it just means this: during execution, JavaScript only runs one instruction at a time. It can't run two instructions at the same time. So when you come across "multi-threaded" versus "single-threaded", you can pretty much picture the difference right away. And the same word shows up on the computer hardware side too, by the way.

Synchronous execution means one instruction at a time — not two, not three — and in a set order. This is the keyword people misunderstand the most. Right about now your gut probably jumps to something called asynchronous requests, but this chapter is just a heads-up about these two concepts, so I'm not going to get into that here.

"Single-threaded" sounds like a fancy, complicated term, but it really just means JavaScript runs one instruction at a time — it can't run two at once. Once you get that, the difference between single-threaded and multi-threaded clicks, and you'll even spot the same word on the hardware side. This post is a quick reminder of two concepts: single-threaded and synchronous execution.
